comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]Midnight is just a few minutes away, and so it's time to launch a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism, and with certain exceptions noted below, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question or comment received after that point will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted.  If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 267,446th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.2 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. 

Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says.  And further:  I've decided that questions about getting goodies from spirits are also permanently off topic here. The point of occultism is to develop your own capacities, not to try to bully or wheedle other beings into doing things for you. I've discussed this in a post on my blog.

The image? I field a lot of questions about my books these days, so I've decided to do little capsule summaries of them here, one per week. As I mentioned last week, once I found a publisher willing to bring out my fiction, a lot of it found its way into print in a hurry, so we're going to be in tentacle territory for a while now.  This was my fifty-third published book, and we're back in The Weird of Hali. This book had the longest and most roundabout genesis of all my tentacle novels. I'd originally planned for the sixth book in the sequence to be set in Greenland, and I wrote six drafts of that novel before realizing that there was too much story to fit into the limits I'd defined for the Weird. So I set the Greenland story aside -- it appeared later, much amended and with different characters, as A Voyage to Hyperborea -- and wrote this one, drawing heavily on the handful of stories Lovecraft set in New York City.

Justin Martense, the central figure in The Weird of Hali: Chorazin, became the viewpoint character in this story, and gave me the chance to explore a heroic fantasy with a very unheroic main character; I later did the same thing to an even greater extent with Toby Gilman, the main character of A Voyage to Hyperborea, who's even more of a dweeb than Justin but rises to the challenges before him in his inimitably awkward way. If you're wondering why I put dorky characters into these two books, why, it's the same reason I made an utterly unheroic sixty-year-old college professor coping with terminal cancer the main character of The Weird of Hali: Dreamlands; I'm bored to tears by the specially special protagonists -- and did I mention that they're special? -- who infest so much fiction these days, and wanted to explore the much more interesting (to me) situation of ordinary people caught up in extraordinary situations. If that turns your crank, why, you can get a copy here if you're in the US and here elsewhere.

Buy Me A Coffee

Ko-Fi

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use either of the links above to access my online tip jar; Buymeacoffee is good for small tips, Ko-Fi is better for larger ones. (I used to use PayPal but they developed an allergy to free speech, so I've developed an allergy to them.) If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:1]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

***This Magic Monday is now closed, and no more comments will be put through. See you next week!***

comments.title: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
comments.content:
Good Evening,

Now that the moon is waning, may whatever you'd have less of wane in your life.

To Share: For any interested in comparative Indo-European mythology, you might find my thoughts on Jaan Puhvel's imaginatively named Comparative Mythology of interest: https://jpowellrussell.com/#book_thoughts_on_comparative_mythology

To Ask: As I've likely mentioned before, by nature I lean pretty heavily to the warmer end of the elements (Fire and Air), but besides my general efforts to better balance myself, I've had some indications that I'm not handling the watery and earthy sides of things as I should, and ineffectively trying to use my more comfortable tools instead (including a hit-me-over-the-head obviously symbolic dream, where my books were ruined by rain and a sprinkler!). Specifically, while I feel like I've gotten better at paying attention to watery and earthy stuff (like how I'm feeling or what's going on with my body), I'm still somewhat at a loss about how to "do" things in a watery or earthy way. Maybe that's the whole point (these are the two more passive/receptive elements, after all), but it seems like my attempts to take (intellectual or will-based, and thus Air or Fire) action in response to what I'm learning from paying attention to my feelings and body (Water and Earth) are falling flatter than I'd like. So, besides daily SOPs, meditation, and divination, to cultivate balance, any tips on how to handle things in a more watery or earthy way?

Thank you again, JMG and everyone here!

My blessings to all who welcome them,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
replies.content: The fact that you're worrying about "doing" is another aspect of the imbalance you're talking about. Water and earth are receptive, not active, so attuning to them involves less action and more reception.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
replies.content:
I was afraid that might be the answer :) Thank you nonetheless, I'll keep working on it (or rather, keep trying to let myself be worked on, as it were).

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
replies.content: That's exactly it. Don't work on it. Stop trying to make it happen, and instead just let it happen. Relax -- that's a water thing -- and wait -- that's an earth thing.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
replies.content: Being pregnant for the second time, I’ve been thinking about this lately. I used to get a little uncomfortable about femininity being associated with receptivity, as it seemed sort of “second class” to me, as if the receptive side of things isn’t actually doing anything and therefore doesn’t really count or get any credit. Being pregnant and giving birth has made it very obvious that I can play a vital role in an incredible act of magic and creation without needing or even being able to consciously direct the process. Of course you can do things to tweak conditions favorably, but ultimately you don’t make any of the specifics happen. You’re not using your will to make them grow their ears or heart valves or whatever. And it’s also pretty clear that “relax and wait” in the case of gestation is neither a negligible contribution nor in any way less important than the, ahem, seminal contribution of the active principle. I don’t know if this example will help you, Jeff, but it has really helped me to internalize that not every important development that I am trying to foster in my life is dependent on or even aided by active striving.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
replies.content:
Hi Jennifer,

Thanks very much, this does help, and is a welcome perspective quite different from my own. Due to my own arrangement of bodies in this incarnation, I've largely been able to sidestep having to confront/embrace such an obviously passive/receptive, but also so obviously important and worthwhile, role in my life. I'm used to waiting or receiving being things to be, at best, accepted as "rules of the game" to be worked within, but not the "focus." So, again, this is a nice, concrete example of what such an attitude leaves out (albeit not one I can go give a try to experience myself!).

Thank you again, and I hope the pregnancy is going well and you've been able to deal with the energetic/spiritual unpleasantness you've been faced with lately,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Comparative Mythology and Question on "Doing" Things in a Watery or Earthy Way
replies.content:
Thanks for saying this! "Passive" and "receptive" so often seem like negative (undesirable) terms. But it occurs to me that learning is a receptive process. We can't learn if we're not listening and receiving ideas and information. And learning is such a major part of what we're doing here.

I've also noticed that I get much more out of a massage when I consciously relax and decide to receive that energy. I've had so-so massages and wonderful massages, and the difference might be more in me than in the therapist. Healing works the same way. You have to be open, willing, to receive it.

replies.content:
Here's another unsettling dream. I visited an island called Laiti, a remnant of the British Empire somewhere in the Pacific. It was set up as an adventure tourism place with lots of international visitors. At some point, most of the visitors left but some of us couldn't get a boat out of there for some reason. We hung out on the beach and partied with the staff of the island, who chose some of us (the ones they most liked) to come to a party in a dance hall. I felt gross smothering vibes and did some praying, got a little bowl of vinegar and they dissipated after a while, but I've felt a bit lower on vitality than usual for the past few days after. An upgrade of my magical protections is in order, apparently.

comments.title: A relevant dream?
comments.content:
Maybe I've been reading the covid threads too much, but this one from last night shook me up a bit. It felt much clearer and more 'dense,' in a way, than the dreams I typically have.

I was with a group of close friends, a kind of 'found family,' who were all practicing goetia. I was afraid to practice with them, so I was just observing. Some of them thought they were doing proper magic, others didn't, but didn't mind because they were getting away with it and getting their way. Then the demons decided they wanted something in exchange for their efforts, and started taking things from the group. Some got very ill, some went catatonic, but one started having body parts taken from him, little by little. I saw that it was Yuval Noah Harari, and he had only his index fingers and half of his ring fingers left on each hand; the rest were bloody stumps, still raw. He was shaking and laughing slightly, going mad from regret. He turned to me with a wild look in his eyes and said, "I hate it."

Thanks as always for these spaces and discussions.

Andy T.

replies.title: Re: A relevant dream?
replies.content: A very unsettling dream. Have you considered journaling to see what it means to you?

replies.title: Re: A relevant dream?
replies.content:
I try to journal every day. I started adding notes on my dreams again a few weeks ago after seeing it mentioned here, and they've gotten more vivid since then. This one stood out though.

In terms of what it means specifically to *me*, there are a few main things that are hard for me to look at.


One is the feeling of having to sit back and watch while people around me do things I find nonsensical and dangerous, without being able to do or say anything about it (or risk losing the relationship entirely). It's been building up since even before 2020, but overwhelming to me now. I try to frame it as preserving the relationship and respecting other people's autonomy as I want them to respect mine. But depending on the situation, I do wonder if there are consequences for me for not speaking up, personally and professionally. Maybe it's worth the ire.

Harari is not someone I know much about, but represents technocratic arrogance and snide materialism to me. (He also looks just like another Israeli vegan who made my life difficult some time back...maybe that's part of why I saw him and not someone like Fauci.) I realize I'm waiting for reckoning and consequences for people who knowingly do harm while feeling a lot of fear for those who got wrapped up in things unknowingly but still cling to the narrative. Facing that level of anger in myself is unsettling. Maybe I should have said "I hate it, too"...

Also, thanks to everyone for the feedback downthread-- I was not aware of those symbols/coincidences! Will have to look into those.

Interesting, I was discussing this with a colleague just last Wednesday, in the context of cleansing and purifying a place as separating and disconnecting it from undesirable influences. If an undesirable person leaves my place and I don't wish them to return, I make sure to rid the place of any of their personal concerns, especially anything that they've used often enough to have their 'imprint,' or has their body fluids. Using such a personal concern to forcefully attract or manipulate a person without their consent is counter to ethical magical practice, but two friends or lovers could deliberately establish such a connection.
Likewise, if I move away from a place and don't wish to return, I make sure not to leave any such things, and to deliberately disconnect from anything left there. I'm careful about wearing anyone's used clothing or shoes. I speculated that perhaps opportunities to travel to a new place could be opened by sending one's own personal concern to a trusted accomplice in the target region, to affix in a spot conducive to travel connections, but I've not experimented with that.
Such ideas are found Southern conjure and folk magic. My grandfather, a Southern Christian folk practitioner, used to send people cloth torn from shirts he'd wear while praying intensely, to conduct a blessing energy, perhaps influenced by Acts 19:12. Likewise in certain Tibetan lineages, inner offering pills made in a sort of homeopathic way, using substances including fragments of clothing worn by realized gurus, or other objects of theirs; some of them go back hundreds of years. These are placed in alcohol, then the yogi tastes drops of it each morning, to connect with the lineage blessings, spiritual realizations and attainments of the masters of a particular lineage or practice transmission. Muslim Sufi orders use the khirqa initiatory cloak to receive the baraka or blessing of the master, and connect a disciple to the spiritual genealogy of their orders: 'by donning a garment that has been worn, or even touched, by the blessed hands of a master, the disciple acquires some of the baraka, the mystico-magical power of the sheikh.' So physical contact with objects may have many uses!

replies.title: Love and Strife
replies.content:
It seems like there are a lot of ways to interpret what Empedocles taught about the roles of what are translated as “Love” and “Strife” in the operation of the cosmos. It’s important, I think, to note that the Greek words he used are not “Agape (as pure selfless Love)” nor “Eris (as chaotic Strife for the sake of strife),” but rather “Philia” and “Neikos.” Neikos has the sense of 'quarrel / disagreement / separation.' In the context of his cosmogonic ideas, I’d interpret these terms as “Affinity / Unity” and “Separation / Individuation.” The word ‘neikos’ may be the root of the word ‘nike’ or victory, which perhaps adds some more depth to the concept of ‘neikos.’

Here’s what the Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy says about these forces in Empedocles’ thought:
“Empedocles also posits two cosmic forces which work upon the elements in both creative and destructive ways. These he personifies as Love (Philia) – a force of attraction and combination – and Strife (Neikos) – a force of repulsion and separation. …these two forces are engaged in an eternal battle for domination of the cosmos and that they each prevail in turn in an endless cosmic cycle. …when Love is completely dominant she draws all the elements fully together into a Sphere in which, although the elements are not fused together into a single mass, each is indistinguishable from the others. The Sphere then, is an a-cosmic state during which no matter can exist, and no life is possible. Then as Love’s power gradually weakens and Strife begins to grow in power, he gradually separates out the elements from the Sphere until there is enough separation for matter to come into existence, for the world to be created and for all life to be born. When Strife has achieved total domination we again get an a-cosmic state in which the elements are separated completely and the world and all life is destroyed in a Whirl. Then Love begins to increase in power and to draw the elements together again, and as she does so the world is again created and life is again born. When Love has achieved full dominance we return once more to the sphere.” https://iep.utm.edu/empedocles/

It seems to me that neither of these forces alone—all-engulfing Philia nor all-repulsing Neikos, centripetal and centrifugal forces—are conducive to biological life nor to relationships among living beings. We need an ever-adjusting balance between the generative, nurturing, collective force of Philia and the striving, individuating, overcoming force of Neikos.

To me, this seems much like forces of Nature in action, and in terms of a spiritual / philosophical path that honors both these roles, one among a number of possibilities is outlined in our host’s excellent book “Mystery Teachings from the Living Earth: An Introduction to Spiritual Ecology.”

In terms of traditional religions there are many possibilities in religions where warriors / strivers / agonistes / heroes on the path of individuation are honored along with nurturing and generative figures. Mithraism comes to mind, traditional Heathen and Celtic spiritual paths, and many others. Heathenry includes Odin’s chosen warriors, the einherjar, the ‘ones who fight alone,’ and the Celts honor similar figures. These heroes constellate the idea of Neikos as ‘separation,’ ‘strife,’ ‘contest,’ and ‘victory,’ yet they also value Philia: the oaths and bonds that tie them to their leaders, comrades, kin, and tribe, and the healldream, the joys of the hall, with feasting, song, gift-giving and fellowship.

Each force has its place in the generation and maintenance of life, spirit, humanity, and the complex, messy world of Midgard!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.title: Re: Love and Strife
replies.content: Thank you, Winifred, I appreciate all this food for thought! Pointing out how Empedocles thought of these two concepts makes things clearer as I untangle how different traditions think about, and value, these things.

replies.title: Re: Love and Strife
replies.content:
Also on a Heathen note, the description there reminds me somewhat of Niflheim and Muspelheim, though obviously with ice being a less seemingly pleasant conceptualization of the force of contraction/unity than love.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Love and Strife
replies.content:
Agreed, Jeff--I think it's interesting how Empedocles' ideas are very similar to many other cosmogonies, philosophies and mythologies positing polarities: Order vs. Chaos, Fire (accelerating energies) vs. Ice (stillness, inertia), and others. Yet Empedocles' idea has an intriguing twist, because rather than static, pre-existing states, he posits *behaviors*, actions, things that not only describe cosmogonic forces but also can be willed into being by human actors and Deities. ...I'd better cut this short here to make the EST deadline!

Winifred

comments.title: For Maxine -- women by the bed
comments.content:
Dear Maxine,

I was unable to respond to your question in time last Magic Monday, but here is a potential explantion for your appearance by the bed of your friend’s wife:

When a woman’s husband (or a man’s wife, though this phenomenon is reported more frequently by women) is strongly sexually attracted/involved with another woman, a female personage can appear to the wife during sleep. Typically it looks like the woman is standing by the bed. It can seem as though the woman is taunting the wife. Sometimes wives arise (in their waking dream—not physically) and pursue the other woman around the house, or even beat her. I am not sure if this is an etheric or astral phenomenon, so I cannot explain how this happens exactly, only that it appears to be in the actual room or space where the wife is sleeping.

But crucially—the woman the wife perceives standing by the bed during sleep does not intend to haunt the wife. And the face that appears on the woman by the bed is not necessarily the face of the woman the man is in lust with—typically the face the wife perceives is that of a woman of the same age as the woman the husband is involved with, but whom the wife dislikes.

The phenomenon was a common reason women were accused of hexery in Pennsylvania Dutch society. I suspect it’s pretty common, even today.

Best wishes,

—Ms. Krieger

comments.title: Dementiaand end of life...
comments.content:
Hello,
As I get older, and watching the previous generations go, I have a big question about the phenomenon of dementia, in the many forms it takes. Some may have entirely functional bodies, only the brain is rotten, some are entirely incapable of anything. I notice I tend to perceive dementia a form of punishment. I do not know what they are being punished for. This theory may be wrong, as my cat reached 18 years, she started having all kinds of weird symptoms, that I only afterwards realized matched pretty well with dementia. I can't think a cat would be getting a punishment for some wrongdoing, but what do I know of cat ethics? But I would like to find a better perspective on the phenomenon, as there is a lot of different levels of forgetfulness, confusion and anxiety going around now. What is the inner significance, or is there any? Of course, dementia forces you to be passive - although some people in care homes are extremely actively trying to get out to go "home", often a childhood home that does not even exist.

There are instructions for the dying we know from many cultures, books of the dead, which require some capacity to consciously direct attention. The soul will be feeling lost, I assume, if the consciousness is gone. Would you have anything to say about this?
Kristiina, today taking the shape of Écru Funicular Flea

replies.title: Re: Dementiaand end of life...
replies.content:
I don't think dementia, or any other illness, is punishment. It's simply a breakdown in the body's ability to serve the purposes of the soul. Back before I got into print, I used to work in nursing homes, and tended a lot of demented patients. My take is that what happened in their case is that the astral body broke down, so that there was an interruption between the soul and the lower bodies -- intermittent or partial in the case of those who are confused and forgetful, complete in the case of those who go into a vegetative state.

As for the dying process, most people die without the chance to focus their attention; it's very common for people to be asleep or unconscious at the time of death. The instructions in the various Books of the Dead or artes moriendi (that's the medieval Latin term for them) are for those fortunate cases where the dying person remains conscious -- though it's wise to read the instructions aloud to the dying person even if he or she seems unconscious, because hearing is the last sense to go and sometimes the message will get through anyway.

replies.title: Re: In the Dreamlands
replies.content:
Hi Eagle Fang,

I see that my grammar left room for misinterpretation-- to be clear, it was a friend's dream featuring my family; but the 7-5-3 ceremony this weekend was real.

As an ex-atheist myself, who was also raised without religion, I'll just offer my own opinion that when people don't feel an urge to connect with the subtler sides of reality, it's generally what is right for them at that point in their journey, and it's best not to urge them in any particular direction. I understand that things are made more complicated when there develops such a misalignment in worldview with someone who is otherwise sharing your own life journey as your partner.

Regarding our host's beliefs regarding Shinto's potential to flourish in North America, although I'll allow that some heavily mutated form of it might make inroads. The difficulty-- but I suppose, depending on how things progress, potentially a benefit as well-- is that historically, Shinto has not been a proselytizing religion. Arguably it's not even a religion at all. I recently spoke with some Mormons who were mystified by Shinto's basic lack of dogma and doctrines (which basically amount to "stay clean, purify your surroundings, connect with divinity" but which provides no formal structure to teach any of this). Basically Shinto provides a ceremonial structure for positive interaction with the spiritual world, including forces indifferent or hostile to humanity as much as those that work with or love humanity. I suppose there is even some way that Shinto could eventually come to fit around other existing religions in the way that Buddhism fits around Shinto-- giving a ritual structure around which positive relationships can be formed with the local Kamisama. Certainly practitioners of Shinto generally see no inherent problem in also giving respect or worship to the deities and holy people of other cultures.

I can't speak for your wife as she's on her own journey. Perhaps it's appropriate just share that in my own case, my sterile agnostic (but basically atheistic) view of the world did not let much oxygen into my universe, and I felt there wasn't much meaning to the world. It was really reading JMG and being impressed by his general reasonableness on matters both spiritual and mundane that gave me license to go ahead and just attempt a spiritual experiment of my own. I picked Shinto as the vehicle for the experiment because I was in Japan; and I believed, as in the parable of the 6 blind men and the elephant, that even if any religions were right, it would be ridiculous to assume one had all the answers and the others had none. To my surprise, my experiment was wildly successful, showing me personal evidence that completely convinced me that there was more in Heaven and Earth than could be dreamt of in my philosophies, and so, here we are nearly 12 years later. Shinto happens to be the first door that opened when I knocked on it; and one of the things I appreciate about it is that is absolutely no pressure from anywhere for me to treat it as the be-all end-all.

comments.title: Architecture
comments.content:
In the past, you have about the effects of architecture in the public space. I kinda agreed with you at the time, but it went to the back of my "ponderings" for a while as I had other things to think about.

But two things came to the fore lately and I thought that I would get your take on the relationship of public architecture to the zeitgeist of our current world. When I go to see my adult children on the other side of the Columbia River, I take public transportation (Orange/Yellow Max) through downtown Portland.

During the last set of tantrums affectionately referred to as "mostly Peaceful Demonstrations", I noticed a couple of things, the first is that the modern buildings got more attention from the protesters than the original buildings. The City Hall, the original federal buildings, and the old courthouses were pretty much left alone. The more modern buildings got much more attention in terms of vandalism.

Then my sister sent me this little X post (she is hooked on the system, where I see it more as a problem than a solution, but even that is a 50:50 deal)

https://x.com/i/status/1858158430219108731

So my question to you is: Buildings, even modern architecture, is pretty long lived. If brutalism and the garbage spewed out by I.M. Pei are contributors to the problem, what can be done to change that. These buildings are going to be around for a while

replies.title: Re: Architecture
replies.content:
I’m interested to hear JMG’s response here.

I will chime in to say Tucker Carlson is right on. Studies have shown that people behave badly in and around modern architecture. When strangers are asked for help to find a lost set of keys, for instance, they are more likely to refuse in a modernist environment.

replies.title: Re: Architecture
replies.content:
First, of course you're right that such buildings bring out the worst in people. Harmonious and beautiful architecture encourages harmonious and beautiful thoughts and actions; hideously ugly and brutal architecture...well, you can do the math yourself.

Second, modern architecture actually doesn't hold up well to the pressures of time. Most modern architects ignored the traditional rules for making buildings last just as much as they ignored the rules for making buildings beautiful. Many of the architectural "triumphs" (that is to say, travesties) of the early 20th century are already gone, and the others -- why, the wrecking ball is on its way.

It would not surprise me, in fact, if the blowback against the managerial state resulted, maybe a decade from now, in a sustained campaign to demolish the hideous architectural eyesores that have been inflicted on our communities, and replace them with neoclassical buildings or the like. Can you imagine the crowdfunding possibilities? Imagine that each city ran a vote to choose which ugly public buildings to destroy, and a crowdfunding campaign to raise the money to bring down the wrecking ball. That would be hugely popular.

comments.title: Thoughts on Pluto
comments.content:
I hadn't really thought about Pluto while walking my dogs, but those walks have a way of popping ideas into my head 😁 and so it happened that one of those ideas struck me as a possible way of reconciling your observation of the "Pluto effect" (things under this planet-oid starting with a bang and ending with not much to show for), and my conviction that this doesn't mean that Pluto itself will stop having any effects on us, just because he's been demoted from planet status.

The thing that is most symbolic of Pluto is radioactivity - after all, two years after he was discovered, humans split the atom, and fifteen years after Pluto's discovery, they dropped the first atomic bomb (I think that was when Oppenheimer invoked Shiva). And it seems to me that radioactivity is a very good model for how Pluto operates.

Radioactivity, according to Wikipedia, is the result of radioactive decay — a process by which an unstable atomic nucleus loses energy by radiation. The decaying nucleus produces daughter nuclei with a different number of protons or neutrons from the parent nucleus, and this is called nuclear transmutation. When the number of protons changes, an atom of a different chemical element is created. This element can also be unstable and therefore create more daughter elements, until the whole decay chain ends in a stable, i.e. radioactively inert element.

So, going by the above, I’d say that any process, event, or thing associated with Plutonian energy or under Pluto’s influence will have the following characteristics:

- it will have been unstable in nature from the start (though that quality will typically be invisible to an observer — some hidden weakness);
- it will radiate energy and seem to be very creative, since under its influence, many new phenomena (“elements”) will suddenly emerge;
- whatever had come under this influence initially will be transmuted (which will often, in the human and social spheres, look exactly like decay, corruption, perversion, or sickness) beyond recognition;
- and most importantly it’s a self-terminating process that will stop when there is no instability left that can be corrupted. At the end of that decay chain, nothing of the original thing will be left.

Now, nuclear decay as well as nuclear fission (where a nuclus splits into two) releases a lot of energy, which is used for example in nuclear power plants, but also in nuclear weapons. So that’s the payoff of associating with Plutonian energy — the incredible power burst that fueled the totalitarian mass phenomena (Faschism, Stalinism) in the 30s when Pluto was discovered. The price is the above mentioned ruination of the one channeling that power, and the eternal poisoning left in its wake.

Since human nature and thus, all human endeavors, have weakness aka sinfulness — the potential for corruption — baked into them, Pluto will unfailingly find this hidden instability under the sign he transits. He’ll hone in to that weakness and start the decay chain, and he’ll whip up obsessions of such titanic strength that nobody can escape their grip. Only when everything that can be corrupted, has been corrupted — when every weakness has been devoured until what’s left has been “transmuted” (perverted) beyond recognition — will that process stop. It has literally run out of fuel, and we collectively rub our eyes and wonder how we could ever buy into that madness and be swept up in its frenzy. That's the point where we realize that for all the sound and fury, there isn't really much to show for, and that all those promises of unlimited power and total transformation into something superhuman were indeed empty.

So yes, whatever great new idea Pluto has fuelled will eventually fizzle out without delivering on its promise; that is the very nature of Pluto, if I’m correct about my nuclear decay model. I just don't think that Pluto will turn this MO on itself, and fizzle out accordingly.

What I find worse than the “much ado about nothing” effect is the irreversible corruption and decay of the things he touches in that process, and how the aftereffects are very similar to nuclear waste: a poisoning of those ideas that Pluto championed, for a very long time. Nobody in the West will ever associate the swastika with anything other than the Nazis, to cite the most obvious example. Maybe there is a way to harness or “vibe harmoniously” with Pluto’s energy, but I don’t see it.

In fact, I don’t think humans are or will ever be fit to deal with the energies of the transpersonal planets; and I wonder if looking out with our telescopes beyond the orbit of Saturn wasn’t an act of transgression that we’re going to pay for until our civilization has turned to dust, and our descendants have lost the knowledge of the existence of those planets. Some things should not become known to humans, and Saturn may well have been the line that shouldn’t have been crossed.

Anyway, that's my take on it. I'm curious what you - as well as the commentariat - think about it. You once mentioned that you began having doubts about Pluto when he transited some points in your chart, and none of the expected effects happened. Going by my theory, maybe those points didn't have any weaknesses to exploit (to start the decay chain). Not saying that you don't have weak points that could be corrupted - every human has them - but maybe these specific ones weren't it, and given how slowly Pluto moves, maybe he won't reach any of those weak points within your lifetime.

replies.title: Re: Thoughts on Pluto
replies.content:
Re "vibing harmoniously", there was that one 1973 book "Minerva or Pluto: The Choice is Yours", if you haven't at least read a summary yet. Once the nature of an instability has been understood, it becomes an avenue for control. Pluto is generally thought to be exalted in Leo.

Re corruption, DNA is, for understandable reasons, pretty hard to purposefully alter much without destroying the cell entirely. However, sometimes you want to do that. Most mutations are harmful changes, but all beneficial changes are mutations. Maybe the important thing is to screen the changes afterwards for long-term effects.

Re coming to terms with the transpersonal planets more generally, you might be interested in the tarot of the surreal fabulist going by the pen name Uel Aramchek. It started as an idle art project series of tweets, which ended up adding an additional "octave" of trumps. Because of his particular artistic priorities and tastes relating to modernity and horror, the trumps end up referencing (in my reading) certain collective processes corresponding to the individual processes from the ordinary trumps. Most of these collective processes are dysregulated and consequently malefic, I guess because they don't have enough "somebody home" yet to animate them in a responsible and reflective way. Sometimes great leaders do a little of that. Sometimes we try to achieve this by "running studies" and have experts who use those studies to guide public policy, but there seems to be some kind of inadequacy with that process.

https://laysfarra.com/tarot.html

For example, The Fool becomes "XXIII: The Network, representing the crossing paths of a thousand fools at what each each thought was the end of their journey." The Tower becomes "XXXIX: The Black Iron Prison. A tangled heap of crash-test dummies fills the space between a radio tower's haphazard trusses."

(There were plans to add the other cards, but I don't know what happened with that. I half-remember for myself imagining Certificates for Coins, Firearms for Wands, Styluses for Swords, and for Cups I think it was Bottles? I think the planned project had Pills instead.)

Re lingering contamination: Materially, the important thing about radionuclides is that enough of them burn out neither quickly (so that contaminated items become safe to use again within a predictable time) nor super slowly (so that the effects are outpaced by forms of turnover that were going to happen anyway).

Re the mechanism of action: The dissident right used to argue that the left (of their time) was unjustly farming influence and power from a process of antinomian burning of those parts of the cultural commons which were norms of restraint. The claim was that the left was repeatedly following an emergently self-reinforcing tactic of accumulating activists and donors by associating its subcultures and institutions with the individual-level short-term libertine gratification from breaking free of conservative subcultures and institutions, especially in colleges ('monasteries for progressivism'), while refusing to pay or even acknowledge the systemic costs that would be inflicted on everyone later on. There's a converse argument that the right refused to pay or even acknowledge the costs of individual-level suffering and ruin resulting from the restraints they coerced people into internalizing, while profiting by gaining tithes and self-flagellating supporters. But the point I wanted to get to is that the argument on the right-wing side provides a little bit of possible mechanism for how your proposed Pluto would be able to act.

comments.title: parasocial relations
comments.content:
As I'm sure you've noticed, with the rise of web 2.0, social media etc, there's been an increase in parasocial relationships -- in which people bond with a famous or well-known person, who knows them little or not at all in return.
I'm wondering what sort of magical dimensions there might be to this. Of course, anything with enough thought and willpower behind it will be tinged with magic, but in this particular case, you have long-term attention and emotional energy directed at a real person, for better or worse. So I'm thinking that lots of love and popular support would have beneficial effects, and that other responses, including fear, anger, or sexual energy, would each empower them in a different way.
Am I on the right track here, and can you elaborate?

replies.title: Re: parasocial relations
replies.content: There have always been parasocial relationships. Fandom was just as frantic before social media -- go find some video of young women going apeshale over the Beatles sometime. The great problem with it is that the target of adulation becomes overcharged with energy, and has to ground it out somehow; unless the person has a decent amount of magical knowledge, obsessive sexuality and alcohol are usually involved.

replies.title: Re: parasocial relations
replies.content: True, this dynamic has always existed. I guess the only real difference is that becoming the object of parasocialism has become more accessible, especially for young women. But anyway, that makes sense, and could help explain some of the more extreme antics of well-known personalities.

comments.title: Hawk
comments.content:
Recently I saw what I believe to be a hawk alighting on a tree branch outside my domicile. The hawk mostly just stood perched there, staring off to the west, but periodically glancing directly at me. It stayed still long enough for me to get several photos of it, and I almost feel as if it wanted me to get photos of it (as I'm not an avid birdwatcher and would need to do so to confirm what kind of bird it was).

Hawks are native to my area, but it's rare seeing one at my residence, and I am wondering if it might be an omen of something. Geomancy on this subject ("was the hawk an omen?") yielded RW: Puer, LW: Albus, J: Via, which seems like a "yes" and also a "change is coming," possibly a martial change given the connotations of Puer and hawks. Am I on the right track or completely off base?

For what it's worth, hawks have no special symbolic meaning to me personally other than keen perceptiveness and their cultural connotation as being associated with warfare as opposed to doves/peace.

replies.title: Re: Solar versus Lunar Path
replies.content: Okay, there are three options, not two -- solar, telluric (earth), and lunar, which combines the two.
